Q1.notes: Iteration 2 SUCCESS: 16% improvement over baseline (164.4ms → 137.9ms). Validation passes with correct results. Scan_filter time reduced from 89.4ms to 64.5ms (28% improvement). Currently 27% slower than DuckDB best (86.4ms) and 60% faster than PostgreSQL (15.3s). Gap to DuckDB: 51.5ms.

Q1.bottleneck: I/O-bound scan on HDD with high thread parallelism causing random seeks
Q1.root_cause: The query scans 60M rows from HDD with high filter selectivity (98% of rows pass). Iteration 2 fixed the critical precision bug and improved performance, but scan_filter still dominates at 64.5ms (46.8% of total). Current implementation uses 64 threads with 100K morsel size, creating excessive random seeks on HDD. The code uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint (line 86), but with 64 threads each accessing different file regions simultaneously, this triggers conflicting kernel read-ahead that causes seek thrashing. Additionally, despite zone maps being available for l_shipdate (storage design shows lineitem sorted by l_shipdate with zone map index), the code doesn't load or use zone map metadata to skip blocks.

analysis.overall: Iteration 2 is a SUCCESS with 16% improvement over baseline (164.4ms → 137.9ms) and correct results. The critical integer precision bug from iteration 1 has been fixed - scaled integer arithmetic now preserves decimal precision. Performance breakdown: scan_filter 64.5ms (46.8%), decode 0.1ms, aggregation 0.0ms, output 0.2ms. Key improvements from baseline: scan_filter reduced 28% (89.4ms → 64.5ms). The dominant bottleneck remains I/O on HDD, but there's still a 51.5ms gap to DuckDB's best time (86.4ms). Main optimization opportunities: (1) Reduce thread count from 64 to 8-16 for HDD to improve sequential access patterns, (2) Increase morsel size to 500K-1M rows to reduce seek overhead, (3) Remove MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint which conflicts with parallel access on HDD, (4) Load and utilize zone map metadata to skip blocks (though 98% selectivity means limited benefit), (5) Consider AVX-512 SIMD (hardware supports it but code uses AVX2).

performance_optimizations.technique: HDD-optimized thread count and morsel sizing
performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation uses 64 threads (std::thread::hardware_concurrency() at line 163) with 100K morsel size (line 164). On HDD with 98% filter selectivity (nearly full scan of 60M rows), 64 threads create excessive random seeks as each thread reads a different region. Reduce thread count to 8-16 to balance parallelism with sequential I/O, and increase morsel size to 500K-1M rows to ensure each thread does substantial sequential work before seeking. SPECIFIC CHANGES: Line 163: 'const size_t num_threads = std::min(16UL, std::thread::hardware_concurrency());' Line 164: 'const size_t morsel_size = 500000;' This change will reduce HDD seek overhead and improve per-thread sequential access patterns, trading some CPU parallelism for I/O efficiency.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25ms reduction in scan_filter time (20-35% improvement) by reducing HDD thrashing. Total time: 137.9ms → 113-123ms, closing gap to DuckDB.

performance_optimizations.technique: Remove MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint for parallel HDD access
performance_optimizations.description: Line 86 uses 'madvise(ptr, size, MADV_SEQUENTIAL)' which tells the kernel to aggressively read-ahead sequentially. This works well for single-threaded scans, but with 64 threads accessing different file regions simultaneously, it causes conflicting read-ahead streams that trigger excessive seeks on HDD. For parallel access on HDD, use MADV_RANDOM or no hint to let threads work independently without kernel interference. SPECIFIC CHANGE: Line 86: Replace 'madvise(ptr, size, MADV_SEQUENTIAL);' with '// madvise(ptr, size, MADV_RANDOM); // Let threads work independently on HDD' OR simply remove the madvise call. This allows the OS to use default page-in behavior without aggressive read-ahead that conflicts between threads.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10ms reduction in scan_filter time (8-15% improvement) by eliminating conflicting kernel read-ahead patterns. Combined with priority 1: total time → 108-118ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Upgrade AVX2 SIMD to AVX-512 for wider vectorization
performance_optimizations.description: Hardware supports AVX-512 (workload_analysis.json shows 'simd_support: avx512') but current code uses AVX2 (line 20: #include <immintrin.h>, lines 170-191 use __m256i for 8-wide int32 SIMD). AVX-512 can process 16 int32 values per instruction (2x wider), reducing loop iterations by half. SPECIFIC CHANGES: (1) Change line 170 from '__m256i cutoff_vec = _mm256_set1_epi32(cutoff_date);' to '__m512i cutoff_vec = _mm512_set1_epi32(cutoff_date);' (2) Update SIMD loop to process i + 16 instead of i + 8 (line 181), (3) Use _mm512_loadu_si512, _mm512_cmpgt_epi32_mask (returns 16-bit mask directly), (4) Update bitmask extraction. AVX-512 provides cleaner comparison semantics with mask registers. This requires careful testing for correctness.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-8ms reduction in scan_filter time (5-12% improvement) by doubling SIMD width. Combined total: 105-115ms. Note: AVX-512 may reduce CPU frequency on some CPUs, potentially offsetting gains.

performance_optimizations.technique: Load and utilize zone map metadata for block skipping
performance_optimizations.description: Storage design shows lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate with zone map index 'lineitem_shipdate_zonemap' (storage_design.json line 31). Zone maps store min/max per block, allowing entire blocks to be skipped if their range doesn't overlap the predicate. Current code doesn't load the zone map metadata file. For this query (l_shipdate <= 10471), with 98% selectivity, zone maps can skip the newest ~2% of blocks (those with min > 10471). Implementation: (1) Load lineitem_shipdate_zonemap.json from gendb directory, (2) Check each block's (min, max) before processing, (3) Skip blocks where block_min > 10471. With 100K rows per block and 60M rows total (600 blocks), this could skip ~12 blocks, saving 1.2M row checks. HOWEVER: With 98% selectivity, most blocks will still need processing, so benefit is limited. More valuable for future queries with lower selectivity (like Q6 with 1.9%).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1-3ms reduction in scan_filter time (2-5% improvement) for Q1 specifically. Greater benefit for more selective queries. Worth implementing as infrastructure for future queries.

performance_optimizations.technique: Perfect hash array instead of dictionary lookups in hot path
performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation already uses perfect hash for final aggregation (lines 91-107) but still does dictionary lookups in the hot path (lines 203-204: 'char ret_flag = returnflag_dict[returnflag_codes[idx]]; char line_status = linestatus_dict[linestatus_codes[idx]];'). These hash map lookups happen 58.8M times (98% of 60M rows). Since l_returnflag has only 3 values (N=0, R=1, A=2) and l_linestatus has 2 values (O=0, F=1), we can use direct array indexing instead of unordered_map. SPECIFIC CHANGES: (1) Replace lines 115-137 with const arrays: 'const char returnflag_dict[3] = {'N', 'R', 'A'}; const char linestatus_dict[2] = {'O', 'F'};' (2) Dictionary lookups become direct array access (no hashing). This eliminates ~117M hash map lookups (2 per row) from the hot path.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5ms reduction in total time (2-4% improvement) by eliminating hash map overhead in hot path. Worth doing for code simplicity even if benefit is small.

performance_optimizations.technique: NUMA-aware memory allocation for thread-local aggregation
performance_optimizations.description: System has 64 cores across 2 sockets (L3 cache description shows '2 sockets'). Current implementation allocates thread-local aggregation arrays without NUMA awareness (line 167: 'std::vector<std::array<AggregateValue, NUM_GROUPS>> local_aggs(num_threads);'). On multi-socket systems, if a thread on socket 1 accesses memory allocated on socket 0, it incurs remote memory access latency (~2-3x slower than local). For optimal performance, use NUMA-aware allocation (libnuma) to ensure each thread's local_agg is allocated on its local NUMA node. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Include <numa.h>, (2) Detect thread's NUMA node at thread start, (3) Allocate local_agg using numa_alloc_onnode(). This requires libnuma library and more complex setup.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-7ms reduction in total time (2-5% improvement) by reducing remote memory access latency for thread-local aggregation. Benefit depends on NUMA configuration and memory binding.

performance_optimizations.description: Current SIMD filtering implementation (lines 194-230) extracts a bitmask and then uses a scalar loop with branching to check each qualifying row (lines 194-230: 'if (qualifies)'). This introduces branch mispredictions. Modern approach: use SIMD gather/scatter or compress operations to process qualifying rows without branching. AVX-512 provides _mm512_mask_compressstoreu which can pack qualifying elements densely. Alternatively, pre-compute qualifying indices in batches and process them with vectorized loads. This is a significant refactoring but eliminates branches in the hottest path.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-6ms reduction in scan_filter time (3-9% improvement) by eliminating branch mispredictions. Complex change with medium risk of introducing bugs.

summary: Iteration 2 SUCCESS: 16% improvement over baseline (164.4ms → 137.9ms) with correct results. The critical integer precision bug has been fixed using scaled integer arithmetic. Performance is now within 60% of DuckDB's best (86.4ms) with a 51.5ms gap remaining. The dominant bottleneck is scan_filter (64.5ms, 46.8% of total) driven by I/O on HDD with suboptimal parallelism settings. Top optimization priorities: (1) Reduce thread count to 8-16 and increase morsel size to 500K-1M for HDD efficiency (expected 15-25ms gain), (2) Remove MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint for parallel access (5-10ms gain), (3) Upgrade to AVX-512 for 2x wider SIMD (3-8ms gain). Combined, these could reduce total time to 100-115ms, closing the gap to DuckDB significantly. Lower priorities include zone map utilization, perfect hash array optimization, NUMA-aware allocation, and branch-free SIMD processing.
